title: Synapse — browser deep-learning lab
description: A deep-learning framework built from scratch on a real reverse-mode tensor autograd engine (no TensorFlow.js) — 30+ hand-derived ops including conv2d, max/avg pooling, LayerNorm, BatchNorm, dropout, GELU/SiLU, embedding, a full softmax Jacobian, and a fused BCE-with-logits + closed-form Gaussian KL, every one proven correct by a one-click engine self-test that gradchecks it (and a whole Transformer, a whole VAE AND a whole diffusion denoiser end-to-end) to ~1e-7. Eighteen labs share the engine: a 2-D Playground (sketch an MLP, train it live on spirals/checkerboards with AdamW/Nesterov, schedules and clipping, and watch decision boundaries, neuron feature maps, train-vs-val curves and a live computation graph); a Vision lab that trains a from-scratch CNN on a fully procedural image dataset (handwritten-style digits 0–9 and shapes) with live filter/feature-map views, a confusion matrix and a draw-your-own pad; a Transformer · Attention lab that trains a from-scratch decoder-only GPT on procedural algorithmic tasks (copy/reverse/sort/add) and shows the live per-layer, per-head causal attention maps, greedy decoding coloured against ground truth, a type-your-own decoder with confidence bars, and a token-embedding PCA; a Generative · VAE lab that trains a from-scratch variational autoencoder (reparameterization trick + KL) on those glyphs and renders a live navigable 2-D latent manifold of synthesised digits, side-by-side reconstructions, a class-coloured latent-space scatter, samples drawn from the prior, latent interpolation between two glyphs, and a slider-driven latent explorer; a Diffusion · DDPM/DDIM lab that trains a from-scratch denoising diffusion model on those glyphs — a time-conditioned residual-MLP that predicts the noise (the Ho et al. ε-MSE objective) over a linear or cosine schedule, with classifier-free label dropout — and lets you watch the headline reverse trajectory (a glyph condensing out of pure Gaussian noise step by step, with the model's live x̂₀ prediction beside it and a scrubber), sample with DDIM (fast, deterministic, η-tunable) or ancestral DDPM, steer with classifier-free guidance, browse a class-conditional sample sheet, spherically interpolate between two noise seeds through the deterministic DDIM map, and read the noise schedule (ᾱ_t / β_t / log-SNR) off a plot — with the denoiser gradchecked end-to-end and the schedule, posterior and DDIM identities proven to machine precision; and a Control · RL lab that trains a from-scratch policy-gradient agent (REINFORCE, REINFORCE+baseline, advantage actor–critic with GAE, and PPO with the clipped surrogate, multi-epoch minibatch SGD and clip-fraction/approx-KL/explained-variance diagnostics) on four hand-written environments — CartPole and a GridWorld maze (discrete), plus Pendulum (a continuous-torque swing-up driven by a diagonal-Gaussian policy with a learnable log-σ) and MountainCar (sparse reward made learnable with optimality-preserving potential-based shaping) — and shows the live agent acting frame-by-frame, the episode-return + policy-entropy learning curves, a per-batch return-distribution histogram, the live action distribution (a μ ± σ gauge for continuous control), and an env-specific portrait (the CartPole phase-portrait, the GridWorld value heatmap, the Pendulum torque field, or the MountainCar position×velocity map). Continuous control added rowSum, gaussianLogProb and gaussianEntropy — all hand-derived and gradchecked alongside whole-policy end-to-end checks for both the discrete and the continuous actor, for 48 self-tested ops in all. The newest lab — Flows · RealNVP — completes the generative trio (the VAE optimises a lower bound, diffusion learns a score, and a normalizing flow gives the EXACT likelihood): a from-scratch RealNVP coupling flow trained by exact maximum likelihood on 2-D densities (two-moons, pinwheel, spirals, circles, a Gaussian grid, checkerboard), where the change-of-variables log-determinant is hand-derived in closed form (the Jacobian is triangular, so log|det| is just the sum of the coupling log-scales) and proven against a finite-difference Jacobian, the flow's exact invertibility f⁻¹(f(x))≡x proven to 1e-16, and the whole stack gradchecked end-to-end through its negative-log-likelihood — visualised with the EXACT model density painted live as a heatmap as it pours into the data's shape, the bijective coordinate warp of latent space into the data manifold, the data's pushforward z=f(x) relaxing onto the base Gaussian's σ-rings, samples drawn the other way x=f⁻¹(z), and a live train/val NLL (and bits/dim) curve. The eighth lab — Graph · GNN — adds the first non-vector modality: a from-scratch graph neural network doing semi-supervised node classification, where only a handful of nodes per class are labeled and the network labels the whole graph by passing messages along edges. Three convolutions share the engine — GCN (the symmetric-normalized propagation Â=D̃^(-½)(A+I)D̃^(-½)), GraphSAGE (a mean neighbor aggregator), and multi-head GAT (graph attention, the per-edge score masked to real edges and row-softmaxed) — each a few dense matmuls against a precomputed propagation matrix, so every gradient is gradchecked end-to-end (GCN/SAGE/GAT all to ~1e-8, the self-test now 54 ops). It runs on procedural graphs (a Stochastic Block Model, the real Zachary Karate Club, and kNN geometric graphs over moons/rings/blobs/spirals) whose node features are deliberately a weak class signal in noise — so the headline is the graph itself, every node filled with its predicted class and ringed with its true one (a colour mismatch is a live mistake) healing as message passing sharpens the labels, with GAT edges glowing by attention; a 'use the graph' toggle collapses the model to a per-node MLP (SBM ~96% test accuracy with the graph vs ~54% without), beside a live PCA of the learned embeddings and train/val/test accuracy curves. The ninth lab — KAN · Splines — is a from-scratch Kolmogorov–Arnold Network (Liu et al., 2024), the architecture that moves the nonlinearity from the nodes to the edges: every connection carries a learned univariate function φ(x) = a SiLU base + a B-spline, and a node just sums. The whole layer is one fused autograd op whose hand-derived backward differentiates the output w.r.t. the base weights, every spline coefficient, AND the input x — the chain rule through the analytic B-spline derivative B′(x) that lets KAN layers stack (gradchecked, input path included, to ~1e-6; the self-test now 59 ops). The headline is the iconic KAN diagram — the network drawn as a graph of functions with each edge's spline rendered inline and animating as it trains, unimportant edges fading as the net prunes itself, click-to-inspect any φ with its knots marked — alongside a live 2-D decision boundary (classification) or the learned 1-D curve through noisy data (regression, where a tiny KAN nails sharp steps a same-size ReLU MLP smears, R²≈0.98). And it exposes the architecture's superpower: the B-spline basis is a partition of unity (proven to machine precision) and its coefficients can be re-solved onto a new knot vector by least squares, so a trained KAN can be refined live (×2 grid resolution) or re-centred onto its activation range without forgetting — curves preserved to ~1e-5. The tenth lab — Neural ODE · Continuous depth — replaces the layer stack with a single learned vector field: a from-scratch continuous-depth classifier where dz/dt = f_θ(z, t) and the prediction is head(z(1)), integrated by a hand-written Euler / midpoint / RK4 solver whose every step is built from the engine's own tape ops, so back-propagation runs straight THROUGH the solver and is gradchecked end-to-end (to ~6e-6). It implements the two ideas that made Neural ODEs famous: the ADJOINT METHOD — the O(1)-memory gradient obtained by integrating a second 'adjoint' ODE backwards in time, its vector–Jacobian products taken with the engine's own reverse mode — which the lab proves reproduces back-prop-through-the-solver on a live batch (to ~3e-7, tightening as you refine the step count); and AUGMENTED Neural ODEs (Dupont et al., 2019) — extra zero-initialised state channels that let trajectories leave the plane, because a 2-D ODE flow is a homeomorphism and cannot pull apart concentric rings without crossing trajectories. The headline is a single canvas fusing the terminal decision regions, the time-dependent learned vector field f_θ(·, t) as a quiver you scrub through time, and the live trajectory of every sample point flowing from t=0 → t=1 (depth made into time) — beside a 'lift' view that shows points rising off the a=0 plane into the augmented axis to route around each other, and accuracy/loss curves. RK4 exactness is proven against the closed-form solution of a linear ODE (z(1)=e^A z0, to ~4e-11), the self-test now covering 62 ops. The eleventh lab — Adversarial · GAN — completes the deep-generative quartet (the VAE maximises an ELBO, diffusion learns a score, a flow gives the exact likelihood, and a GAN learns to SAMPLE the data without ever writing down a density): a from-scratch Generative Adversarial Network where an MLP generator G(z) and an MLP discriminator/critic D(x) are trained against each other in a genuinely new two-optimiser, two-objective alternating loop (Adam with β₁=0.5, a configurable critic:generator step ratio). Three objectives expose the training dynamics that ARE the story of GANs: the original saturating minimax (min_G max_D E log D(x)+E log(1−D(G(z)))), the non-saturating log D(G(z)) trick (strong gradients, the default), and a Wasserstein critic trained on E[D(real)]−E[D(fake)] (an Earth-Mover estimate) kept ~1-Lipschitz by weight-clipping — D always trains on detached fakes so the critic update never reaches into G. The headline is the discriminator's DECISION SURFACE painted live on a diverging blue↔amber (fake↔real) ramp with the real data in cyan and the generator's fakes in amber on top, watch the sharp early fence flatten toward ½ as G learns to mimic the data and the amber fakes settle onto the cyan data — the equilibrium of the game made visible — plus an optional generator pushforward of the latent grid into data space; beside it a generated-samples panel where mode collapse is plainly visible and a dual-loss chart (the two losses chasing each other, or, for WGAN, the Wasserstein estimate that actually trends to 0). Both players are gradchecked END-TO-END against finite differences (gan-D ~2e-8, and crucially gan-G ~4e-9 — the gradient the discriminator back-propagates INTO the generator, the GAN learning signal, proven exact), alongside the WGAN critic and an exact mean(D(fake))−mean(D(real)) loss identity, for 66 self-tested ops in all. The twelfth lab — Uncertainty · Bayes — moves past point estimates to CALIBRATED PREDICTIVE UNCERTAINTY, the property production ML lives or dies on (a confidently wrong prediction is worse than none): three from-scratch ways to make a net report how much it doesn't know, all on the one autograd. Bayes-by-Backprop (Blundell et al., 2015) makes every weight a Gaussian q(w)=N(μ, softplus(ρ)²) and trains the ELBO (data-NLL + (β/N)·KL(q‖prior)) with the reparameterization trick; MC-Dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016) keeps dropout ON at test time so each pass is a posterior sample; Deep Ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017) train M independent nets and read uncertainty off their disagreement. Every model predicts a HETEROSCEDASTIC Gaussian (a mean AND a log-variance), so the lab cleanly splits ALEATORIC (irreducible data noise) from EPISTEMIC (reducible model doubt) uncertainty via the law of total variance — and the headline view shows the predictive ±1σ/±2σ bands FAN OUT in the data gap and the extrapolation tails exactly where the model has seen nothing, beside a reliability diagram (calibration + ECE), a spaghetti of sampled plausible functions, and the honest held-out NLL. The two new probabilistic losses (a fused heteroscedastic Gaussian NLL and the variational Gaussian KL), a single Bayes-by-Backprop layer and a whole BBB MLP are gradchecked end-to-end through the ELBO (~1e-9), and the law of total variance is proven as an identity — five new checks. The thirteenth lab — Sparse · Mixture-of-Experts — is the architecture that actually powers today's frontier language models (Mixtral, DeepSeek, GShard, Switch), and the one thing the other twelve labs are NOT: sparse. It's a from-scratch decoder-only Transformer (reusing the exact multi-head causal-attention assembly) whose dense feed-forward is replaced, in every block, by E independent expert MLPs and a tiny linear ROUTER that sends each token to only its top-k experts — so the model's CAPACITY (all E experts) decouples from its per-token COST (k experts), the panel reading off the dividend live (e.g. 6 experts of capacity, ~52% of params firing per token). Top-k routing is done with the elegant softmax(G + topkMask) identity (an additive −1e9 mask makes the softmax renormalise over exactly the chosen k — Mixtral's 'softmax of the top-k logits' — and stays fully differentiable in the router logits G), the experts are mixed by two new hand-derived ops (scaleRows: weight an expert's whole output vector by its per-token router weight; selectCol: pull one expert's gate column), and the router is kept from collapsing onto a favourite expert by the Switch-Transformer LOAD-BALANCING auxiliary loss E·Σ_i f_i·P_i (the dispatch fraction f detached, the mean router probability P differentiable, so the gradient flows through P exactly as in the paper) plus a small router z-loss for logit stability. It trains on the same procedural algorithmic tasks (copy/reverse/sort/add) and makes the three things that make MoE WORK visible and live: the headline ROUTER heatmap (for a worked example, which expert every token is dispatched to in each layer, the renormalised top-k weights shaded and the chosen experts outlined), an EXPERT-LOAD bar against the uniform ideal with a single-figure load-imbalance (coefficient-of-variation) readout that you watch FALL as the aux loss flattens the experts (turn the aux weight to 0 and watch the router collapse instead), an EXPERT-SPECIALISATION matrix (per expert, the token types it learned to take), live token-by-token decoding, and an aux/imbalance curve. The two combine ops gradcheck to ~1e-11 and a WHOLE sparse-MoE Transformer is gradchecked end-to-end through the combined task + load-balancing loss (~3e-5 in the self-test, ~3e-7 on a clean masked-CE probe) — the self-test now covering 74 ops; out-of-browser it solves the sort task to 100% while the load-balancing loss drives the per-expert imbalance from 0.36 to 0.06. The fourteenth lab — Morphogenesis · Neural Cellular Automata (Mordvintsev et al., Growing Neural Cellular Automata, Distill 2020) — is the first whose output isn't a one-shot map but an EMERGENT one: a single local update rule, shared by every cell of a grid, that grows an organism from one seed cell, holds its shape, and regrows what you cut off — trained by BACK-PROPAGATION THROUGH TIME straight through the engine's tape (the loss on the FINAL frame of a 20–40-step rollout flows back through every step). The model is a per-cell perception (a hand-derived depthwise Sobel-x/Sobel-y/identity filter-bank autograd op) feeding a 3C→hidden→C update MLP whose second layer is zero-initialised so the CA starts as the identity and must LEARN to grow, gated by a stochastic async-fire mask and the Distill alive mask (maxpool₃(α)>0.1, pre ∧ post, stop-gradient). Three recipes — Grow (seed every batch), Persist (a sample pool ⇒ a stable fixed point) and Regenerate (pool + random-disc damage ⇒ self-healing) — and eight procedural SDF targets (heart, star, smiley, flower, droplet, ring, spiral, ∇; no bundled assets). The headline is a canvas that grows the creature live (a fast no-tape inference path, machine-precision-identical to the taped trainer) and lets you DRAG TO WOUND it and watch it heal, with a hidden-channel inspector and a live sample-pool strip. The engine self-test grew by three checks (perceive 1e-9, a whole CA rollout gradchecked end-to-end through BPTT 6e-7, and the zero-init-identity), 77 ops in all; out-of-browser, 220 steps grow a heart (MSE 0.022), persist it stably, and regrow it after damage (0.090→0.033). The fifteenth lab — Recurrent · RNN/LSTM — adds the architecture that defined sequence learning before attention, and the one phenomenon none of the other labs exhibit: memory carried THROUGH TIME, trained by BACK-PROPAGATION THROUGH TIME. From-scratch vanilla RNN (h_t = tanh(Wx + Uh + b)), GRU (update/reset gates) and LSTM (input/forget/output gates over a protected cell-state highway) cells are each assembled from the engine's primitive tape ops and unrolled over a sequence one step at a time, so the whole unrolled graph — every gate, across every timestep — back-propagates with the existing optimizer and is gradchecked end-to-end (a new stackRows op glues the per-step logit rows into the [T,V] matrix the shared masked-cross-entropy trains through; the three cell BPTT checks land at RNN 8e-9 / GRU 5e-6 / LSTM 3e-5, the self-test now 81 ops). Four temporal tasks expose what recurrence is FOR: recall a cue symbol across a wall of distractors (the canonical long-range credit-assignment probe), stream the running parity of a bit string (one bit of state the whole run, the textbook task a feed-forward net can't do online), a delayed copy of a whole block, and a character-level language model over a tiny procedural grammar you SAMPLE from with a temperature knob. The headline is the lab's reason to exist made visible: a GRADIENT-THROUGH-TIME plot that back-propagates one long-range loss through a freshly-initialised cell of each type and draws ‖∂L/∂h_t‖ on a log axis against timestep — the plain RNN's gradient COLLAPSES toward the cue (Hochreiter's vanishing gradient, read straight off the engine's tape) while the LSTM's additive cell highway and the GRU's gated carry keep it alive (the LSTM reaching the cue ~20× more strongly at lag 30), the architectural property that predicts which cells can learn long dependencies at all — beside live hidden-state and cell-state heatmaps evolving step by step, per-gate activation heatmaps (watch the LSTM forget gate stay near 1 over distractors — initialised with the classic positive forget-bias), generate-and-check sampling, and held-out predictions coloured by correctness. Out-of-browser the LSTM learns streaming parity and lag-8 recall to 100% accuracy. The sixteenth lab — State-Space · Mamba — adds the modern linear-time rival to the Transformer (Gu & Dao, 2023): a from-scratch SELECTIVE state-space model (S6) whose token mixer is one hand-derived SELECTIVE-SCAN autograd op running the input-dependent diagonal recurrence h_l = exp(Δ·A)·h_(l−1) + (Δ·B)·x_l, y_l = C·h_l + D·x_l — Δ, B and C are projected from the token itself, so the model SELECTS what to write into its state per token and per channel (exactly the content-aware gating a linear-time-INVARIANT SSM like S4 lacks) — assembled into a real Mamba block (RMSNorm, a causal depthwise conv, a SiLU gate) and stacked into a language model that trains in O(L) time and memory with NO positional encoding. Three new hand-derived ops — RMSNorm, the causal depthwise conv, and the selective scan whose full vector-Jacobian product is taken w.r.t. ALL SIX inputs (x, Δ, A, B, C, D) — gradcheck to ~1e-9, the whole Mamba LM gradchecks end-to-end through masked cross-entropy, and the discretization (ā=exp(Δ·A), b̄=Δ·B·x) is proven exact against an exponential-moving-average closed form to 1e-16, the self-test now 86 ops. It trains on the Mamba paper's diagnostic synthetics — SELECTIVE-COPY (reproduce only the data tokens scattered through a long field of blanks, solvable only by content selection) and INDUCTION / associative recall (recall the value paired with a queried key) plus copy and reverse — and makes selectivity legible: the headline Δ HEATMAP (per token × inner channel, how much each input is written into the state), a state-magnitude + mean-Δ trace over the sequence, live token-by-token decoding with confidence, and a one-press in-browser end-to-end gradient check; out-of-browser a 32-dim, 2-layer Mamba solves selective-copy, induction AND reverse to 100%. Save, load and share experiments via a URL link. And now an EIGHTEENTH lab — AlphaZero · Self-play — the first lab that plays a game and learns from NO data at all: one two-headed conv net (a move POLICY head + a position VALUE head) guides a from-scratch PUCT Monte-Carlo Tree Search, and the games the net plays against ITSELF — each position labelled by the search's visit counts and the game's eventual result — are the only training signal (Silver et al., 2017); the improved net makes a stronger search, which makes better self-play, and so on. It is exactly verifiable because Tic-Tac-Toe is solved: a from-scratch perfect negamax + alpha-beta solver is the ground-truth oracle, so the trained agent reaches 0 losses to perfect play (a draw is the game-theoretic ceiling), MCTS handed that perfect evaluator reproduces a minimax-optimal move at EVERY reachable position (4520/4520 in test), and the combined policy-cross-entropy + value-MSE + L2 loss gradchecks end-to-end to ~1e-7. Connect-Four (real gravity, four-in-a-row in every direction, a depth-limited alpha-beta opponent) is the bigger, prettier cousin. You play the LIVE network on an SVG board with the search's per-move visit / value / prior halos drawn right on it and a position value bar, watch a top-moves search table (prior P vs search visits vs backed-up value Q), and see the losses-to-perfect curve fall to zero and the score-vs-random curve climb as it trains — all on the same hand-rolled autograd engine, no MCTS or game library.
